conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. 

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions. Let me start by gathering some basic information.

Do you have any children that you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do! I have a son called Alex. He's 17 years old and lives at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I'll need to ask you a few more questions about your situation to determine your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

First, are you responsible for Alex? This means do you live with him and pay towards the costs of bringing him up (such as food, clothes, or pocket money)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, absolutely. Alex lives with me here in Birmingham, and I pay for everything – his food, clothes, phone bill, all of that. He's my responsibility.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's great, thank you. Now I need to ask about Alex's current situation:

Is Alex currently in approved education or training? For example, is he studying at school, college, or doing an approved training course?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, he actually finished his GCSEs recently and left school. He's not in college or anything right now, but he's signed up with the local careers service to figure out what he wants to do next – whether that's sixth form, an apprenticeship, or something else. He's still working it out.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for explaining that. Let me ask a couple more questions to get a complete picture.

First, when exactly did Alex turn 16? (Please provide the month and year if you can remember.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Let me think... he turned 16 about a year ago, so that would have been around May or June last year, 2024.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you. Just to clarify - you mentioned Alex is 17 and finished his GCSEs recently. Can you tell me approximately when he left school or finished his education? Was it after his 16th birthday in May/June 2024?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right. He finished his GCSEs this past summer, so around June or July 2025. That was after he'd already turned 17.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. Now, a few more important questions:

Are you (or your partner, if you have one) currently receiving Child Benefit for Alex, or have you in the past?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I've been receiving Child Benefit for Alex since he was born. I'm just wondering if I can still get it now that he's left school and is 17.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I understand your concern. Let me ask one final question to complete my assessment:

You mentioned Alex is registered with the local careers service. Has he been officially accepted onto their programme, and is he actively attending appointments or sessions with them? Also, do you know if this careers service is an approved programme under Child Benefit rules?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he's signed up and registered with them properly. He's been going to his appointments – they're helping him look at his options and figure out what path to take. I'm not entirely sure about the official rules around it though – I just know he's actively working with them to sort out his next steps.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for providing all that information. Let me read the official guidance to properly assess Alex's eligibility.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for your patience. Based on the official guidance I've reviewed and the information you've provided, I can now assess Alex's eligibility for Child Benefit.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news!** You are currently eligible to continue receiving Child Benefit for Alex.

Here's why:

Since Alex is 17 and has recently left education (finishing his GCSEs in summer 2025), and he's now registered with a local careers service, you can continue to receive Child Benefit for **up to 20 weeks** from when he left school. This is a special extension period that applies when a 16 or 17 year old leaves education and registers with a government-sponsored careers service.

This 20-week period (running from approximately June/July 2025) would give Alex time to decide on his next steps. 

**Important points to remember:**

- If Alex enrolls in approved education or training (like sixth form, college, or an apprenticeship) before he turns 20, your Child Benefit can continue as normal
- If he doesn't enter approved education or training within the 20-week period, your Child Benefit for Alex would stop at the end of that period
- If Alex starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training, you'll stop getting Child Benefit

Since you're already receiving Child Benefit for Alex, you should continue to receive it during this transition period. It's a good idea to keep track of when Alex makes his decision about his next steps and inform HMRC if his circumstances change.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and in the 20-week extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 17 and in the 20-week extension period; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 17 and in the 20-week extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old and left approved education in June/July 2025. According to Child Benefit rules, you can continue to receive Child Benefit for 20 weeks if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with either a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services. Alex has registered with the local careers service and is actively attending appointments with them. This means you should remain eligible for Child Benefit for Alex for up to 20 weeks from when he left school (approximately June/July 2025). You meet the basic eligibility criteria as you are responsible for Alex (he lives with you and you pay for his upkeep including food, clothes, and other expenses). The 20-week extension period would cover approximately until November/December 2025. If Alex then enrolls in approved education or training (such as sixth form, college, or an apprenticeship) before turning 20, your Child Benefit can continue.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has been receiving Child Benefit for their 17-year-old son Alex since birth. Alex recently left school after finishing his GCSEs in summer 2025 and is now registered with a local careers service. Under UK Child Benefit rules, parents can continue to receive Child Benefit for up to 20 weeks when a 16 or 17 year old leaves education and registers with a government-sponsored careers service. The claimant remains eligible during this 20-week extension period as they are responsible for Alex (he lives with them and they pay for all his expenses). If Alex enters approved education or training before the 20 weeks expires or before he turns 20, Child Benefit can continue.

This means you should remain eligible for Child Benefit for Alex for up to 20 weeks from when he left school (approximately June/July 2025). You meet the basic eligibility criteria as you are responsible for Alex (he lives with you and you pay for his upkeep including food, clothes, and other expenses). The 20-week extension period would cover approximately until November/December 2025. If Alex then enrolls in approved education or training (such as sixth form, college, or an apprenticeship) before turning 20, your Child Benefit can continue.

Alex recently left school after finishing his GCSEs in summer 2025 and is now registered with a local careers service. Under UK Child Benefit rules, parents can continue to receive Child Benefit for up to 20 weeks when a 16 or 17 year old leaves education and registers with a government-sponsored careers service. The claimant remains eligible during this 20-week extension period as they are responsible for Alex (he lives with them and they pay for all his expenses). If Alex enters approved education or training before the 20 weeks expires or before he turns 20, Child Benefit can continue.
